text: The video displays a laparoscopic anterior resection for upper rectal carcinoma in a patient with situs inversus. The laparoscope is positioned laterally to the left of the umbilicus, with surgical ports placed in the left iliac fossa and left upper quadrant for the surgeon's left and right hand operations.

The laparoscopic view shows the setup for an anterior resection of an upper rectal carcinoma in a patient with situs inversus. The laparoscope is positioned laterally to the left of the umbilicus. The surgeon's ports are placed in the left iliac fossa and left upper quadrant for the left and right hand, respectively. An assistance retraction port is located lateral to the umbilicus on the right. The initial laparoscopy reveals the splenic flexure in the right upper quadrant and the liver in the left upper quadrant.

During the initial laparoscopy, the assistance retraction port is positioned lateral to the umbilicus on the right side. The splenic flexure is observed in the right upper quadrant, while the liver is located in the left upper quadrant, reflecting the patient's situs inversus condition.

The surgeon lifts the sigmoid colon out of the pelvis, revealing the tumor located in the upper rectum, marked by a tattoo below it. Adhesions between the omentum and the right anterior abdominal wall are divided.

The surgeon lifts the sigmoid colon out of the pelvis, revealing the tumor in the upper rectum marked by a tattoo. Amental adhesions between the right anterior abdominal wall and the colon are divided. The omentum and transverse colon are reflected upwards, and the small bowel is displaced into the left upper quadrant to expose the operative field.

The surgeon reflects the omentum and transverse colon upwards, displacing the small bowel into the left upper quadrant to expose the operative field. Unusual adhesions between the sigmoid mesocolon and the posterior peritoneum are divided, allowing the sigmoid colon to be elevated out of the pelvis, revealing the sacral promontory.

The surgeon divides unusual adhesions between the sigmoid mesocolon and the posterior peritoneum, elevating the sigmoid colon out of the pelvis to reveal the sacral promontory. Dissection is performed with a harmonic scalpel, starting anterior to the promontory and moving cephalad towards the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect anterior to the sacral promontory, moving cephalad towards the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery. The artery is displayed, controlled with hemlock clips, and divided using the harmonic scalpel.
The surgeon displays the inferior mesenteric artery, secures it with hemlock clips, and then divides it using a harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon elevates the sigmoid colon anteriorly, exposing the gonadal vessels and right ureter. Dissection continues along the taut fascia, mobilizing the descending colon over the perinephric fat until the colon is fully mobilized.

The surgeon elevates the sigmoid colon anteriorly, exposing the gonadal vessels and right ureter. Dissection continues along the taut fascia to mobilize the descending colon, progressing over the perinephric fat until the colon is fully mobilized.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the descending colon by dividing the peritoneum in the right paracolic gutter. After mobilizing the splenic flexure, the focus shifts to the pelvis where dissection begins on the left side of the mesorectum, displacing it forwards, and then continues on the right side, medial to the right ureter.
The surgeon performs lateral division of the peritoneum in the right paracolic gutter, completing the mobilization of the descending colon.

After mobilizing the splenic flexure, the surgeon begins dissection on the left side of the mesorectum, displacing it anteriorly, then proceeds to the right side, medial to the right ureter. The dissection continues within the mesorectal plane until the rectum is fully mobilized.

The surgeon continues dissection in the mesorectal plane, fully mobilizing the rectum. The distal mesorectum is divided, followed by the transection of the rectum at the junction of the middle and lower third.
The surgeon divides the distal mesorectum before transecting the rectum at the junction of its middle and lower third, using precise dissection techniques.

The surgeon exteriorizes and resects the tumor through an umbilical incision. The proximal colon is returned to the abdominal cavity with an anvil positioned. A staple gun is advanced through the rectum near the left lateral end of the staple line.

The surgeon exteriorizes and resects the tumor through an umbilical incision. The proximal colon is returned to the abdominal cavity with the anvil attached. The staple gun is advanced through the rectum near the left lateral end of the staple line, coupled with the anvil, closed, and fired to complete the colorectal anastomosis.
The surgeon couples the anvil and closes the staple gun, firing it to perform the colorectal anastomosis. The surgical field shows the completed anastomosis with no immediate complications visible.
Post-operative recovery was uneventful and the patient was discharged home on the third post-operative day.
